OOP is a way of organizing code that uses classes and objects to represent real world entities and their behavior. Object has attribute thing that has specific data and can perform certain actions using methods. Organizes code into object and classes Supports encapsulation which group data and methods together. Enables inheritance for resusability and heirarchy Allows Polymorphism for flexible method implementation Improves modularity, scalabilty and maintainabilty.

Class is the collection of objects. Class defines a set of attributes and methods which the created objects have. Classes are created by keyword class Attributes are the variable belongs to a class. Attributes are always public and can be accessed by using dot(.) operator. class attribute is shared by all instance of the class. __init__() is a constructor method which runs automatically when a new instance is created.It is used to initialize object data. self represents current object allowing each instance to store and access its own data. instance attribute is unique to each objects of the class. class Dog: species = 'Canine' # class attribute def __init__(self,name,age): self.name=name # instance attribute self.age=age # instance attribute

Object is the instance of the class , each object holds its own data.he objects consists of State : It is represented by the attributes and refelects the properties of an object. Behavior : It is represented by the methods of an object, and reflects the response of an object to other objects. Identity : It gives a unique name to an object and enables one object to interact with other objects. # 1. We create the objects my_phone = Smartphone("iPhone 15") abhishek = Person("Abhishek") # 2. The interaction happens abhishek.call_friend(my_phone)

class Dog: species = "Canine" # Class attribute def __init__(self, name, age): self.name = name # Instance attribute self.age = age # Instance attribute # Creating an object of the Dog class dog1 = Dog("Buddy", 3) print(dog1.name) print(dog1.species) sFour Pillars of OOP
Inheritance allows a class to acquire properties and methods of another class.It promotes code reuse and hierarchical classification. TYPES OF DIFFERENT INHERITANCE

A single child inherits from a parent parent class. class Parent: def feature_one(self): return "Feature 1 from Parent" class Child(Parent): def feature_two(self): return "Feature 2 from Child" obj=Child() print(obj.feature_one()) # Feature 1 from Parent ----- Inherited

This is where a child class inherits from more than one Parent class. NOTE:This is superpower unique to C++, Python .Java doesn't allow this because it can cause problem known as Diamond Problem(Child class wouldn't know which parent's method to run if they both had same name).Python solves this using an order system called MRO(Method Resolution Order). class Father: def skills(self): return "Coding" class Mother: def hobbies(self): return "Painting" class Child(Father, Mother): # Inherits from BOTH pass kid = Child() print(kid.skills()) # From Father: Coding print(kid.hobbies()) # From Mother: Painting

A child class inherits from a parent class , which in turn inherits form a grandparent class. class Grandparent: def land(self): return "Owned 100 acres" class Parent(Grandparent): def house(self): return "Built a house on the land" class Child(Parent): def car(self): return "Bought a car to park at the house" kid = Child() print(kid.land()) # Trickled down from Grandparent print(kid.house()) # Trickled down from Parent

One parent class serves as the base for multiple child classes.The child shares the parent features, but they dont share feartures each other. class Vehicle: def info(self): return "This is a transport vehicle." class Car(Vehicle): def speed(self): return "Top speed 120 mph" class Truck(Vehicle): def cargo(self): return "Can carry 5 tons" # Both Car and Truck get 'info()', but Car doesn't get 'cargo()'

Hybrid inheritnce is a combinatin of two or more inheritance listed above. eg: A mix of Multiple and Multilevel inheritance. class School: def school_name(self): return "High School" class Faculty(School): # Hierarchical/Multilevel branch def work(self): return "Teaching" class Sports: # Independent base def activity(self): return "Basketball" class Student(Faculty, Sports): # Multiple Inheritance branch pass

Same operation but different behavior. It allows different classes to have functions or methods with the same name to work differently depending on the type of object they acting upon. Python handles this smoothly because of python in dynamically typed. class Duck: def sound(self): return "Quack" class Dog: def sound(self): return "Woof!" def make_it_speak(animal_obj): animal_obj.sound() make_it_speak(Dog()) # Woof! make_it_speak(Duck()) # Quak! In Pyhton Polymophism is flexible ,usually shows up two main inheritance. With Inheritance and Without Inheritance.

This is a class-object oriented concept.Multiple child class inherits from a parent class, but they override a specific method to change its behaviour. class Document: def __init__(self,name): self.name=name def show(self): pass class WordDocument(Document): def show(self): return f"Opening {self.name} in MIcrosoft Word Document." class PDFDocument(Document): def show(self): return f"Opening {self.name} as PDF layout." def open_file(obj): print(obj.show()) open_file(WordDocument("Resume.docx")) open_file(PDFDocument("Ebook.pdf"))

Python is dynamically typed,Which gives a unique flaovour of inheritance called ** Duck Typing**.The phrase comes from saying :"If it walks like a duck and quacks like duck , its a duck". Python doesn't care about the type of the class or Family tree it only care abouts the object has the method it wants to call. class Duck: def __init__(self,name): self.name=name def sound(self): return f"{self.name} sounds Quack" class Dog: def __init__(self): self.name=name def sound(self): return f"{self.name} sounds Woof" class CarHorn: def __init__(self): self.name=name def sound(self): return f"{self.name} sounds car" # A function doesn't care about What object it gets,as long as it can make sound def make_sound(obj): obj.sound() make_sound(Duck("maju")) mak_sound(Dog("Tilo")) make_sound(CarHorn("i20")) Here Non_animal also works ,beacuse it can make sound. In Many programming language like Java ,if you want a function to accept a parameter , you have to explicitly pass its exact data type or family tree. So in Java was this it wont work for otherthan what type it mentioned in the function.Suppose for this make_sound only accepts object from Animal class , then it wont accept CarHorn class object.

We using polymophism in everyday, without realizing it. Built-in functions like len() change their behavior depending on what you give them. # len() counts characters in a string print(len("Python")) # Output: 6 # len() counts items in a list print(len([1, 2, 3])) # Output: 3 # len() counts key-value pairs in a dictionary print(len({"a": 1, "b": 2})) # Output: 2
documents.s: C. Polymophism in Built-in Python Functions

documents.t: A. Method Overriding​ Redefining a parent class's method inside a child class. B. Method Overloading​ Having multiple functions in the same class with the exact same name but different input arguments.eg: abc(x,y,z) and abc(x,y). NOTE: Python doesn't support native method overloading.If we write 2 functions with the same name , the second one simply overrides the first one.To mimic overloading we use default args (like def add(x,y,z=9) or variable arguments *args).

Python reads code from top to bottom. When it encounters the second function, it simply points the function name to the new definition, completely erasing the first one. Why can't Python do this? Python is a dynamically typed language and functions don't enforce input data types. Furthermore, a Python class is essentially a dictionary under the hood. When you define a method, Python stores it as a key-value pair: {"add": function_object}. If you define a second method named add, Python simply updates that dictionary key to point to the new function, completely erasing the old one. Java, on the other hand, keeps a structured list of distinct method signatures. class Calculator: # First definition (takes 2 arguments) def add(self, x, y): print("Running the 2-argument version...") return x + y # Second definition (takes 3 arguments) # Python looks at this and says: "Oh, 'add' means THIS now!" def add(self, x, y, z): print("Running the 3-argument version...") return x + y + z calc = Calculator() # 1. Testing the 3-argument version (Works perfectly) print(calc.add(5, 10, 20)) # Output: # Running the 3-argument version... # 35 # 2. Testing the 2-argument version (CRASHES!) print(calc.add(5, 10))

In Java, this code works perfectly. Java supports native method overloading because it uniquely identifies a method by its Signature. A method signature in Java isn't just the name of the function; it is a combination of: The method name. The number of parameters. The data types of those parameters. class Calculator { // Method 1: Signature is "add(int, int)" public int add(int x, int y) { System.out.println("Running 2-argument version..."); return x + y; } // Method 2: Signature is "add(int, int, int)" // Java sees this and says: "This is a brand new method!" public int add(int x, int y, int z) { System.out.println("Running 3-argument version..."); return x + y + z; } public static void main(String[] args) { Calculator calc = new Calculator(); // Java checks the inputs: 2 integers -> runs Method 1 System.out.println(calc.add(5, 10)); // Output: // Running 2-argument version... // 15 // Java checks the inputs: 3 integers -> runs Method 2 System.out.println(calc.add(5, 10, 20)); // Output: // Running 3-argument version... // 35 } } a. Default arg​ class Calculator: # We set z to 0 by default. If the user doesn't provide it, it stays 0. def add(self, x, y, z=0): return x + y + z calc = Calculator() # Mimicking 2-argument overloading print(calc.add(5, 10)) # Output: 15 (z automatically becomes 0) # Mimicking 3-argument overloading print(calc.add(5, 10, 20)) # Output: 35 b. Variable args​ class AdvancedCalculator: # *args will capture every single number passed into a tuple named 'args' def add(self, *args): total = 0 for number in args: total += number return total calc = AdvancedCalculator() # You can now call the exact same method with 2, 3, or even 5 numbers! print(calc.add(5, 10)) # Output: 15 print(calc.add(5, 10, 20)) # Output: 35 print(calc.add(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) # Output: 15 c. Overloading Based on Type​ Sometimes in other languages, you overload a function not because of the number of inputs, but because of their type (e.g., wanting add(int, int) to behave differently than add(str, str)). In Python, you can achieve this inside a single function using isinstance() to check the data type: class Greeter: def greet(self, name): # If they pass a list of names if isinstance(name, list): for n in name: print(f"Hello, {n}!") # If they just pass a single string name else: print(f"Hello, {name}!") welcome = Greeter() welcome.greet("Alice") # Output: Hello, Alice! welcome.greet(["Bob", "Charlie"]) # Output: Hello, Bob! \n Hello, Charlie!

Building of data (attributes) and methods (functions) within a class, restricting to access some components to control interactions. In Python, we do this using underscores to signal that a variable is "private." class BankAccount: def __init__(self, owner, balance): self.owner = owner self.__balance = balance # Double underscore makes it private # Getter method to safely look at the balance def get_balance(self): return self.__balance # Setter method to safely change the balance with validation def deposit(self, amount): if amount > 0: self.__balance += amount account = BankAccount("Alice", 1000) # print(account.__balance) # ERROR! Python protects this. account.deposit(500) print(account.get_balance()) # Output: 1500

Abstraction hides the internal implementation details while exposing only the necessary functionality.It focus on "what to do" rather than "how to do". from abc import ABC, abstractmethod class Vehicle(ABC): # Abstract Class @abstractmethod def start_engine(self): pass class Car(Vehicle): def start_engine(self): return "Car engine started. Vroom!" # my_vehicle = Vehicle() # ERROR: You cannot create an instance of an abstract class directly. my_car = Car() print(my_car.start_engine()) # Output: Car engine started. Vroom!

The Diamond Problem occurs when a child class inheritance from two parent classes, And both of those parent classes inherit from the same grandparent class.If both parents override a method from the grandparent, and the child calls that method, which parents method does it use? [ Grandparent (A) ] -> has a method: clean_room() / \ [Parent B] [Parent C] -> both override clean_room() \ / [ Child D ] -> calls clean_room(). Who wins? B or C?

To prevent confusion, every class in Python has an MRO a strict , flat list that tells Python exactly which order to search for a method. Python searches from left to right. we can inspect by using Class.__mro__

How Python builds that MRO without breaking things? It uses an algorithm called C3 Linearization. C3 enforces three strict rules. Preserve Subclass Order : Children are always checked before parents. Preserve Local Precedence : If you write class D(B,C):, B must always checked before C. Monotonicity : If class B comes before class C in an inheritance chain, it must stay that way across the entire application. NOTE : If you try to design a messy inheritance tree that breaks these mathematical rules, Python will refuse to compile it and raise a TypeError: Cannot create a consistent method resolution order (MRO).

Most People thinks super() just calls the parent class.In sigle inheritance it does. But in Multiple Inheritance, super() actually calls the next class in MRO list, not neccessarily the parent. class A: def process(self): print("A's process") class B(A): def process(self): print("Entering B") super().process() # Calls the next thing in the MRO list print("Leaving B") class C(A): def process(self): print("Entering C") super().process() # Calls the next thing in the MRO list print("Leaving C") class D(B, C): def process(self): print("Entering D") super().process() print("Leaving D") # Let's look at D's MRO: D -> B -> C -> A -> object d = D() d.process() The Output Sequence Notice how C gets executed even though B is not a parent of C! Entering D Entering B Entering C A's process Leaving C Leaving B Leaving D

The ABC class is strict structural contract. It allows you to define a template class that can't be instantiated (writing internal logic or code) on its own, forcing any child classes to implement (writing the instruction) specific methods. If you want to be a subclass of AppPlugin, you absolutely must write the your own run() method. from abc import ABC, abstractmethod class AppPlugin(ABC): # Inheriting from ABC makes it abstract @abstractmethod def run(self): """Every child class must implement this method""" pass class MyPlugin(AppPlugin): # If we forget to write run(), Python will crash when we create the object def run(self): return "Plugin is executing!" plugin = MyPlugin() # Works perfectly!

Introduced to make class customisation simpler. __init_subclass__ is method inside the parent class. It triggers automatically when a new child classs inherits from it . It helps parent class to inspect,validate, or register child classes the exact millisecond they are defined, before you ever create an instance. class Registry: plugins=[] def __init_subclass__(cls,**kwargs): super().__init_subclass__(**kwargs) #Automatically keep list of all subclasses created Registry.plugins.append(cls) print(f"Automatically registered plugin: {cls.__name__}") class AudioPlugin(Registry): pass class VideoPlugin(Registry): pass #Output: #Automatically registered plugin: AudioPlugin #Automatically registered plugin: VideoPlugin

class is blueprint of creating an Object, then a Metaclass is a blueprint for creating a Class. In Python, classes themselves are objects. By default, all classes in Python are built by a master metaclass called type. A custom metaclass lets you intercept and completely rewrite how python constructs a class object at runtime. NOTE : You rarely need custom metaclasses. __init_subclass__ handles 95% of use cases cleanly. Use metaclasses only if you are building massive libraries or deep frameworks (like Django or Pydantic) where you must fundamentally alter Python class structures. Metaclass The blueprint that builds classes, giving you complete control over class modification. # A metaclass inherits from 'type' instead of 'object' class UpperCaseAttributesMeta(type): def __new__(mcs, name, bases, class_dict): # We intercept the class creation and force all attributes to uppercase uppercase_dict = {} for key, value in class_dict.items(): if not key.startsWith("__"): # Leave special dunder methods alone uppercase_dict[key.upper()] = value else: uppercase_dict[key] = value # Let 'type' actually construct the class with our modified dictionary return super().__new__(mcs, name, bases, uppercase_dict) # We link our class to the metaclass using the 'metaclass' keyword class User(metaclass=UpperCaseAttributesMeta): role = "admin" status = "active" u = User() # print(u.role) # CRASH! 'role' doesn't exist anymore. print(u.ROLE) # Output: admin (The metaclass rewrote the attribute name!)
Byte code is a low-level intermediate platform indepedent representation of source code. NOTE:Cpython- software (also call- Python interpretor) = Cpython Compiler+PVM (Cpython interpretation part)

1- Compilation - CPython interpreter(Compilor component of the interpreter) automatically transfers your source code (python code) into byte code 2- Caching - To speed up future runs,the byte code is cached in .pyc file within __pycache__ folder. 3- Interpretation - The PVM reads the byte code instructions line by line and executes on the specific os.

Platform indepedent Efficeincy - Byte code is more compact and faster for the PVM to executes more than raw text. Implementation - The byte code format can change b/w python versions. Abstraction - The byte code isn't meant for CPU, it is meant for software based PVM. Internal instructions - The byte code is looks like series of numeric code in PVM Level Representation What it looks like Who reads it Human Code Source Code a+b You (The Coder) Mnemonic Disassembled code BINARY_ADD The dis module Numeric (OPCODE) Actual Byte code 122(example) The PVM Machine level code Binary 01111010 The RAM \ Transistors Mnemonic (pronounced- ne-mon-ik) is simply memory aid.It is a way to replace something complex with something much easier for human to recognize.

We can see the Byte code of any function using built-in dis (disassembler) module. import dis def add(a,b): return a+b dis.dis(add) output​ 4 0 LOAD_FAST 0 (a) 2 LOAD_FAST 1 (b) 4 BINARY_ADD 6 RETURN_VALUE Output Shows the specific operations the PVM performs: LOAD_FAST : Load variable a onto stack LOAD_FAST : Load variable b onto stack BINARY_ADD : Add 2 values. RETURN_VALUE : Return the result.
For portability - python stays flexible as long as the system has a python interpreter, it can run code reagardless of the hardware.
This portion covers authentication techniques
Ensuring secure access and identity management.

Full form: JSON Web Token A compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties. It allows for stateless authentication, meaning the server doesn't need to store session data in a database to verify the user. NOTE: You can't truly do stateless. You must have a blacklist in memory for various reasons, like logged out users, compromised tokens, etc.

A stateful, server-side storage mechanism where user data is kept in memory or a database, identified by a unique ID. It offers maximum security and control; the server can instantly revoke access by deleting the session from its storage, which is harder to do with stateless JWTs.

Full form: Single Sign-On A session and user authentication service that permits a user to use one set of login credentials to access multiple applications. It creates a seamless User Experience (UX) and reduces "password fatigue" while centralizing security management for an organization. OAuth 2.0 is an industry-standard framework for authorization, allowing apps to securely access user data (on platforms like Google or Facebook) without needing user credentials. While not an authentication protocol itself, it authorizes actions via access tokens. It is often combined with OpenID Connect for authentication.

Full form: Open Authorization 2.0 An industry-standard protocol for delegated authorization, allowing apps to access data on behalf of a user without seeing their password (e.g., "Login with Google"). It limits exposure; a third-party app only gets a scoped token to do specific tasks rather than having full access to your account.

Full form: OpenID Connect An identity layer built on top of the OAuth 2.0 protocol that allows clients to verify the identity of the end-user. It provides a standardized way to handle "Login"; while OAuth 2.0 is about delegating access (authorization), OIDC is about proving identity (authentication).
How do we save / handle the token or session id on the client side?

A method where the server uses a browser's cookie storage to automatically send and receive authentication data (either a Session ID or a JWT) with every request. It is effortless for the developer because the browser handles sending the cookie automatically, and it can be made highly secure using the HttpOnly flag to prevent XSS attacks.

A method where the client manually attaches a token (usually a JWT) to the Authorization header of an HTTP request (e.g., Authorization: Bearer xxx). It is built for mobile apps and cross-domain APIs where browsers might block cookies or where you aren't using a browser at all. The token may be saved in different places depending on the application or device. KeyChain (iOS), Keystore (Android), Local storage (browser) or session storage (browser)
This section covers the mechanics of how you keep a user logged in and how you kick them out when necessary.

A short-lived credential (often a JWT) used by the client to access protected resources. It allows for fast, stateless verification without hitting the database for every single API request. It's often paired with a refresh token

A long-lived credential used solely to obtain a new access token (often a JWT) once the current one expires. It balances security and UX by allowing users to stay logged in for days without keeping a highly sensitive access token active that entire time.

A security pattern where every time a refresh token is used, it is invalidated and replaced with a brand-new one. It detects malicious reuse; if an attacker steals a token and uses it, the real user’s subsequent attempt will fail, alerting the system to revoke the entire session.

Full form: Session Identifier A unique string stored in a cookie that points to a specific data record on the server (in RAM or DB). It provides instant revocation control; if a user’s account is compromised, the admin can delete the session server-side to log them out immediately.
Full form: Time To Live A hard-coded timestamp within a token (the exp claim) that defines when it becomes invalid. It limits the "window of opportunity" for an attacker if a token is intercepted.

The process of maintaining a list of "invalid" JWTs (usually in Redis) that haven't expired yet but should no longer be accepted. It solves the main weakness of stateless JWTs, allowing you to log a user out or ban them before their token naturally expires.

A technique where the expiration time of a session is extended every time the user makes a new request. It keeps active users logged in indefinitely while automatically cleaning up and logging out inactive users after a period of idleness.
This portion covers security related questions & techniques

Full form: Role-Based Access Control An approach to restricting system access to authorized users based on their assigned roles (e.g., Admin, Editor, Viewer). It simplifies permission management; instead of managing permissions for 1,000 users, you manage them for 5 roles and simply assign users to those roles.

Full form: Attribute-Based Access Control A logical access control methodology where access rights are granted through the use of policies which combine attributes (user, resource, and environment). It provides fine-grained control; for example, you can create a rule saying "Only Managers (User Attribute) can view Financial Reports (Resource Attribute) if they are accessing from the office IP (Environment Attribute)."
RBAC is better for smaller, standard applications where roles are clearly defined. ABAC is better for complex, enterprise-scale systems that require high security and context-aware permissions.
How we protect the infrastructure from abuse and high-volume traffic.

A technique to control the number of requests a user or IP can make to a service within a specific timeframe. It prevents DoS/DDoS attacks and ensures that a single "noisy neighbor" (a user making too many requests) doesn't crash the server for everyone else. Rate Limiting algorithms Token Bucket: Users are given a "bucket" of tokens. Every request uses one. Tokens refill at a steady rate. (Allows for "bursts" of traffic). Leaky Bucket: Requests enter a bucket and "leak" out to be processed at a constant, fixed rate. (Ensures a very steady, predictable flow; no bursts allowed). Fixed Window: Limits requests per calendar minute (e.g., 100 requests between 10:00 and 10:01). (Easy to implement but can be "gamed" at the minute boundaries). Sliding Window: A more accurate version of Fixed Window that looks at the exact previous 60 seconds from the current timestamp.

The process of gradually slowing down a user’s responses once they near their limit, rather than hard-blocking them. It provides a better user experience (UX) by allowing a user to finish their task at a slower speed instead of receiving an immediate "429 Too Many Requests" error.

Full form: Web Application Firewall A specialized firewall that monitors, filters, and blocks HTTP traffic to and from a web application. It provides edge-level protection against common attacks like SQL Injection and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) before the traffic even touches your internal network.

A configuration that rejects any incoming request if the body (JSON/Image) exceeds a pre-defined size (e.g., 5MB). It prevents memory exhaustion attacks; without this, an attacker could send a 1GB JSON file that crashes your server’s memory while it tries to parse it.

A network security mechanism that allows (whitelists) or blocks (blacklists) traffic based on the sender's IP address. It secures internal services; for example, a production database should only allow connections from the specific IP addresses of your application servers.

Fail-Closed (Best for Auth): If the auth service is down, the system denies all access. Better to be down than compromised. Fail-Open (Best for UX): If a non-critical feature (like a recommendation engine) fails, the user can still use the core app.

A security strategy that uses multiple layers of defense so that if one layer fails, others are in place to stop the attack. It assumes that no single security measure is perfect; by layering WAF, API Gateways, and Hashing, you make it exponentially harder for an attacker to reach the core data.

Full form: Principle of Least Privilege The practice of giving a user, process, or system only the minimum level of access necessary to perform its job. It minimizes the "Blast Radius"; if a specific service is compromised, the attacker can't move laterally to the rest of the system because that service had restricted permissions.

A security model based on the principle of "Never Trust, Always Verify," even for users or devices already inside the corporate network. It prevents attackers from moving freely inside a network once they get past the initial firewall, which is how most major data breaches escalate.

Encrypting data while it is stored on a physical disk (e.g., in a database or cloud storage). It protects against physical theft or unauthorized access to the server's hard drives or database backups.

Encrypting data as it moves across a network between the client and the server (e.g., via HTTPS/TLS). It prevents "Man-in-the-Middle" (MITM) attacks, ensuring that attackers cannot sniff or alter sensitive data as it travels through the internet.

While often discussed in Performance, Idempotency is a security/reliability feature. Concept: Ensuring that making the same request multiple times (e.g., "Pay $10") has the same effect as making it once. It prevents double-charges or duplicate data entries if a user clicks a button twice or if a network retry happens automatically.

The Problem: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) allows an attacker to run JavaScript in your user's browser. Since Local Storage is accessible via JS, a simple localStorage.getItem() can steal the token. The Mitigation Content Security Policy (CSP): Restrict which scripts can run and where they can send data. Input Sanitization: Never trust user input; encode it to prevent script injection in browsers. The "Gold Standard": Don't store sensitive tokens in Local Storage. Keep the Access Token (JWT) in memory (JS variable) and the Refresh Token in an HttpOnly cookie.
documents.s: Q: If I store my JWT in Local Storage, how do I prevent XSS from stealing it?

documents.t: The Problem: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) tricks a logged-in user's browser into sending an unwanted request (like "transfer money") because the browser automatically attaches cookies to every request to that domain. Anti-CSRF Tokens: Include a unique, hidden token in every state-changing request (POST/PUT) that the server validates. SameSite Cookie Attribute: Set your cookie to SameSite=Strict or Lax so the browser won't send it during cross-site requests.
documents.s: Q: If I use Cookies to store my Session ID, how do I prevent CSRF?

documents.t: HttpOnly: Prevents JavaScript from accessing the cookie (mitigates XSS). Secure: Ensures the cookie is only sent over encrypted HTTPS connections. SameSite: Controls whether the cookie is sent with cross-site requests (mitigates CSRF).
documents.s: Q: What are the essential Cookie security flags?

documents.t: It should be multi-layered. At the Edge (WAF/CDN): To block massive DDoS attacks. At the API Gateway: To manage per-user/per-key quotas. At the Service Level: To protect specific "expensive" database queries or third-party API calls.
documents.s: Q: Where should you implement Rate Limiting?

documents.t: The Answer: Choose ABAC when access depends on context—like time of day, location, or specific resource ownership—rather than just a job title. Example: Think of RBAC as a VIP list at a club (Are you on the list? Come in). Think of ABAC as a bouncer checking your ID, your sobriety, and if you’re wearing the right shoes (context matters). Example: A healthcare app where a "Doctor" (Role) can only view a "Patient Record" (Resource) if they are the "Assigned Physician" (Attribute).
documents.s: Q: When would you choose ABAC over RBAC in a real-world project?

documents.t: The Answer: Bursts. Token Bucket allows for a sudden burst of requests as long as there are tokens in the bucket. Leaky Bucket enforces a strict, uniform output rate regardless of the incoming pressure. Why it matters: Use Token Bucket for APIs where users might occasionally need to load a lot of data at once; use Leaky Bucket for background processing tasks where you want to ensure your database never sees a spike.
documents.s: Q: What is the biggest practical difference between the Token Bucket and Leaky Bucket algorithms?

documents.t: The Answer: Rate Limiting is a "brick wall" (429 error); Throttling is "molasses." It degrades the user experience gracefully by slowing down the response time rather than breaking the integration entirely. Why it matters: This prevents a client’s automated retry logic from slamming your server the microsecond the rate-limit window resets (preventing the "thundering herd" problem).
documents.s: Q: Why is Throttling considered "kinder" than simple Rate Limiting?

documents.t: The Answer: No. This is called Defense in Depth. A WAF catches broad, known patterns (like common SQL injection strings) at the edge. The Peer Perspective: Your application still needs to validate business logic that a WAF can’t see—like ensuring a user isn't trying to withdraw $1,000 from an account with a $50 balance.
documents.s: Q: Does a WAF (Web Application Firewall) make my application-level validation redundant?

documents.t: The Answer: It prevents Memory Exhaustion DoS attacks. An attacker could send a massive, multi-gigabyte JSON "bomb" that forces your server to use all its RAM during parsing, crashing the service for everyone. NOTE: Mention that this should be configured at the Nginx/Load Balancer level so the "fat" request never even reaches your app code.
documents.s: Q: Why is Payload Size Limiting considered a "security" feature and not just a performance one?

documents.t: The Answer: For the public side? No, because users have dynamic IPs. But for internal communication (e.g., your Admin Panel only accepting traffic from your office VPN), it is an incredibly effective "lock" that stops random internet scans.
documents.s: Q: Is IP Whitelisting a valid security strategy for a modern, public-facing Web App?

documents.t: The Problem: Hardcoding API keys or DB passwords in your source code is a major security risk. The Solution: Use Environment Variables or dedicated Secret Managers (like AWS Secrets Manager). The Peer Insight: Secrets should be encrypted and injected into the application at runtime, never checked into Git.
documents.s: Q: How do you handle "Secrets Management" in a production environment?

documents.t: Full form: HTTP Strict Transport Security A security header that tells the browser to only communicate with the server via HTTPS, even if the user types http://. It prevents SSL Stripping attacks, where an attacker tries to downgrade your connection to unencrypted HTTP to steal your cookies.
documents.s: Q: What is HSTS and why should we care?
